title: Tetanus
oneLiner: Rare but life-threatening bacterial toxin causing generalised muscle rigidity and painful spasms; occurs in unvaccinated, elderly, or contaminated wounds.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction from laryngeal spasm**  
  - *Single finding:* Sudden inspiratory stridor, cyanosis, or inability to speak — this is not “just anxiety” or “croup.” Requires immediate jaw-thrust and call for senior anaesthetist / ICU.  
  - *Needs:* Protect airway with benzodiazepines first; if fails, rapid sequence induction (RSI) with suxamethonium (use high dose due to muscle rigidity? – check local policy) and intubation.
- **Autonomic storm / dysautonomia**  
  - *Single finding:* Labile blood pressure (hypertension → hypotension), brady/tachyarrhythmias, hyperpyrexia, and profuse sweating — within minutes to hours. Can cause cardiac arrest.  
  - *Needs:* ICU admission, beta-blockade (e.g. labetalol), and magnesium sulphate as first-line stabiliser.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Trismus (lockjaw) – inability to open mouth >3 cm
  - Risus sardonicus – sustained smile from facial muscle rigidity
  - Generalised muscle rigidity (abdominal, paraspinal, chest wall)
  - Painful, sudden spasms triggered by minor stimuli (touch, noise, light)
  - Opisthotonos – arching of back during severe spasms
  - Autonomic instability: tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis, fever
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Jaw stiffness (trismus) – the single earliest sign in 80%
  - Spasms on palpation or auscultation (e.g. stethoscope on chest)
  - Preserved consciousness throughout
  - May find a wound (not always obvious – look for puncture, burn, bite, surgical site, IV drug use track)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Dental abscess / peritonsillar abscess** – trismus only, no generalised rigidity, no reflex spasms, localised swelling/pain
  - **Drug-induced dystonia (e.g. metoclopramide, antipsychotics)** – acute onset, torticollis, oculogyric crisis, no trismus; responds dramatically to benztropine
  - **Meningitis / encephalitis** – neck stiffness + photophobia, fever, altered consciousness, Kernig/Brudzinski signs; no trismus or sustained rigidity
  - **Strychnine poisoning** – identical spasms but history of ingestion, no trismus initially (or milder), rapid onset <1h
  - **Hypocalcaemic tetany** – Chvostek/Trousseau signs, paraesthesiae, no trismus, no wound
- **Splitter:** *Sustained trismus with generalised rigidity and stimulus-induced spasms = tetanus; pure trismus with no other signs = dental abscess.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Wound inspection (any break in skin, even trivial; look for foreign body, puncture, burn)  
  - Spatula test: touch back of throat with spatula – in tetanus, jaw clenches instead of gag reflex (positive)  
  - Check vaccination record / history
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U+E, LFT, CK (rhabdomyolysis risk), CRP  
  - Blood cultures (if wound source)  
  - Calcium, magnesium, phosphate (exclude metabolic tetany)
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT head / neck (if trismus alone, to rule out retropharyngeal abscess)  
  - X-ray or CT of suspected wound (look for radio-opaque foreign body)  
  - Chest X-ray (to assess aspiration, rib fractures from spasms)
- **Special tests:**  
  - No specific confirmatory laboratory test – diagnosis is **clinical** (tetanus toxin can be detected in wound/serum but not routinely available)  
  - Wound culture – positive in <30% cases; do not wait for results
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Check immunisation history** – if unknown or incomplete, proceed to full management (IG + vaccine). Missing this delays passive immunisation and vaccine boost.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Airway assessment** – prepare for intubation if any stridor, hypoxia, or respiratory compromise  
  - **Stop spasms** – benzodiazepines (diazepam IV 5–10 mg, repeated; or midazolam IV infusion)  
  - **Wound management** – clean & debride wound thoroughly (remove devitalised tissue, foreign body)  
  - **Passive immunisation** – human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG) [check local policy for dose: usually 250 IU IM for prophylaxis; for established tetanus 3000–6000 IU IV or IM]  
  - **Active immunisation** – tetanus toxoid vaccine (0.5 mL IM) at a different site  
  - **Antibiotics** – metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS (or benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV QDS)  
  - **ICU referral** – immediate if any generalised spasms, trismus severe, or airway compromise
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - **ICU/HDU** – all confirmed cases: monitor for autonomic storms, muscle spasms, respiratory failure  
  - **Sedation** – titrate benzodiazepines (diazepam or midazolam) ± propofol; consider magnesium sulphate infusion (loading 40 mg/kg, then 1–2 g/h IV to maintain 2–4 mmol/L serum Mg)  
  - **Muscle relaxants** – morphine for sedation and sympathetic blockade; baclofen (oral or intrathecal) if severe  
  - **Autonomic management** – beta-blockers (propranolol, labetalol) for tachycardia/hypertension; atropine for bradycardia; magnesium as first-line  
  - **Nutrition** – nasogastric feeding (or parenteral if ileus); high-calorie, high-protein due to catabolism  
  - **Aspiration prevention** – head-up position, suction, consider PEG if prolonged
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Complete tetanus vaccination series (if not immune): 3 doses of tetanus toxoid (at 0, 1, 6–12 months)  
  - Rehabilitation – physiotherapy, speech therapy (dysphagia), psychological support
- **Default Orders (if admitted – ICU/HDU):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s at 1 mL/kg/h (titrate to BP, avoid overload)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) – use with caution if bleeding risk from trauma  
  - Diet: NBM until swallowing assessed (aspiration risk); start NG feed once stable  
  - Observations: continuous cardiac monitoring, SpO2, hourly BP, temperature; record NEWS2 every 15 min initially, then 1-4 hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG)** – [check local policy] dose for treatment: 3000–6000 IU IV (or IM if IV unavailable); give ASAP  
  - **Tetanus toxoid (adsorbed)** – 0.5 mL IM (deltoid)  
  - **Metronidazole** – 500 mg IV (or PO once able) TDS for 7–10 days  
  - **Benzodiazepines:**  
    - Diazepam 5–10 mg IV bolus (can repeat q10 min), then infusion 5–10 mg/h titrated  
    - Midazolam 1–5 mg IV bolus, then infusion 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/h (ICU only)  
  - **Magnesium sulphate** – 40 mg/kg IV loading over 30 min, then 1–2 g/h infusion; target serum Mg 2–4 mmol/L
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV QDS (if allergy to metronidazole)  
  - Propofol infusion (ICU sedation)  
  - Baclofen 10–40 mg PO QDS (or intrathecal pump in refractory spasms)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Benzodiazepines – respiratory depression (monitor O2 saturations)  
  - Magnesium – causes hypotension, muscle weakness; contraindicated in myasthenia gravis  
  - Beta-blockers – avoid in acute bradycardia; use labetalol with caution
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Metronidazole: adjust in severe hepatic impairment (reduce dose by 50%)  
  - Magnesium: reduce infusion rate in renal impairment (monitor serum Mg)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving HTIG IM without wound debridement** – toxin still present; debridement is essential before IG works.  
  - **Overdosing benzodiazepines** causing respiratory arrest – always have resuscitation equipment ready.  
  - **Using suxamethonium for RSI** – can cause hyperkalaemia in muscle rigidity states; prefer rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV (if available).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Respiratory failure (laryngospasm, chest wall rigidity, aspiration pneumonitis)  
  - Autonomic instability (arrhythmias, hypotension, cardiac arrest)  
  - Rhabdomyolysis (leading to AKI)  
  - Fractures (vertebral compression, rib fractures from opisthotonos)  
  - Venous thromboembolism (immobility)  
  - Hyperpyrexia (from sustained muscle activity)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Prolonged ICU stay (weeks to months)  
  - Dysphagia, voice changes  
  - Post‑ICU weakness, tracheostomy dependence in severe cases
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality: 10–20% overall; higher in elderly, neonates, and those with short incubation period (<7 days).  
- Recovery is slow; survivors often have full recovery but may require months of rehabilitation.  
- Autonomic instability is the leading cause of death.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about vaccination history** – even if patient says “I’ve had them all,” check the red book or GP records.  
- **Debride the wound thoroughly** – even if it looks clean; remove any foreign body and devitalised tissue.  
- **Start benzodiazepines early** – don’t wait for severe spasms; titrate to relaxation without respiratory depression.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t attribute trismus to dental abscess without examining for generalised rigidity** – you’ll miss tetanus.  
- **Don’t give IM HTIG into the gluteal muscle** – use deltoid or lateral thigh; avoid injecting near identified wound.  
- **Don’t forget active immunisation** – tetanus disease does NOT confer immunity; give toxoid at the same visit.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – o:
- **Return if:**  
  - Any jaw stiffness or difficulty opening mouth  
  - Unexplained muscle aches or stiffness in neck/back  
  - Fever, chills, or new wound erythema/swelling  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP in 2–3 days for wound check and to complete tetanus vaccination series (if incomplete)  
  - If HTIG was given, repeat toxoid at 1 month and 6–12 months (if primary series)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  - Progression of trismus or any generalised spasms  
  - Stridor, respiratory distress, or SpO2 <92% on room air  
  - Autonomic instability: BP >160/100 or <90/60, heart rate >130 or <50, temperature >39°C  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single parameter ≥3)  
- **Call crash team:**  
  - Cardiac arrest, severe bradycardia, or hypotension unresponsive to fluids  
  - Suspected laryngospasm with airway obstruction  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (if available)  
  - GCS <14 (rare, but can occur from sedation or hypoxia)

rawMarkdown:
## Tetanus
**One-liner:** Rare but life-threatening bacterial toxin causing generalised muscle rigidity and painful spasms; occurs in unvaccinated, elderly, or contaminated wounds.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction from laryngeal spasm**  
  - *Single finding:* Sudden inspiratory stridor, cyanosis, or inability to speak — this is not “just anxiety” or “croup.” Requires immediate jaw-thrust and call for senior anaesthetist / ICU.  
  - *Needs:* Protect airway with benzodiazepines first; if fails, rapid sequence induction (RSI) with suxamethonium (use high dose due to muscle rigidity? – check local policy) and intubation.
- **Autonomic storm / dysautonomia**  
  - *Single finding:* Labile blood pressure (hypertension → hypotension), brady/tachyarrhythmias, hyperpyrexia, and profuse sweating — within minutes to hours. Can cause cardiac arrest.  
  - *Needs:* ICU admission, beta-blockade (e.g. labetalol), and magnesium sulphate as first-line stabiliser.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Trismus (lockjaw) – inability to open mouth >3 cm
  - Risus sardonicus – sustained smile from facial muscle rigidity
  - Generalised muscle rigidity (abdominal, paraspinal, chest wall)
  - Painful, sudden spasms triggered by minor stimuli (touch, noise, light)
  - Opisthotonos – arching of back during severe spasms
  - Autonomic instability: tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis, fever
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Jaw stiffness (trismus) – the single earliest sign in 80%
  - Spasms on palpation or auscultation (e.g. stethoscope on chest)
  - Preserved consciousness throughout
  - May find a wound (not always obvious – look for puncture, burn, bite, surgical site, IV drug use track)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Dental abscess / peritonsillar abscess** – trismus only, no generalised rigidity, no reflex spasms, localised swelling/pain
  - **Drug-induced dystonia (e.g. metoclopramide, antipsychotics)** – acute onset, torticollis, oculogyric crisis, no trismus; responds dramatically to benztropine
  - **Meningitis / encephalitis** – neck stiffness + photophobia, fever, altered consciousness, Kernig/Brudzinski signs; no trismus or sustained rigidity
  - **Strychnine poisoning** – identical spasms but history of ingestion, no trismus initially (or milder), rapid onset <1h
  - **Hypocalcaemic tetany** – Chvostek/Trousseau signs, paraesthesiae, no trismus, no wound
- **Splitter:** *Sustained trismus with generalised rigidity and stimulus-induced spasms = tetanus; pure trismus with no other signs = dental abscess.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Wound inspection (any break in skin, even trivial; look for foreign body, puncture, burn)  
  - Spatula test: touch back of throat with spatula – in tetanus, jaw clenches instead of gag reflex (positive)  
  - Check vaccination record / history
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U+E, LFT, CK (rhabdomyolysis risk), CRP  
  - Blood cultures (if wound source)  
  - Calcium, magnesium, phosphate (exclude metabolic tetany)
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT head / neck (if trismus alone, to rule out retropharyngeal abscess)  
  - X-ray or CT of suspected wound (look for radio-opaque foreign body)  
  - Chest X-ray (to assess aspiration, rib fractures from spasms)
- **Special tests:**  
  - No specific confirmatory laboratory test – diagnosis is **clinical** (tetanus toxin can be detected in wound/serum but not routinely available)  
  - Wound culture – positive in <30% cases; do not wait for results
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Check immunisation history** – if unknown or incomplete, proceed to full management (IG + vaccine). Missing this delays passive immunisation and vaccine boost.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Airway assessment** – prepare for intubation if any stridor, hypoxia, or respiratory compromise  
  - **Stop spasms** – benzodiazepines (diazepam IV 5–10 mg, repeated; or midazolam IV infusion)  
  - **Wound management** – clean & debride wound thoroughly (remove devitalised tissue, foreign body)  
  - **Passive immunisation** – human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG) [check local policy for dose: usually 250 IU IM for prophylaxis; for established tetanus 3000–6000 IU IV or IM]  
  - **Active immunisation** – tetanus toxoid vaccine (0.5 mL IM) at a different site  
  - **Antibiotics** – metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS (or benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV QDS)  
  - **ICU referral** – immediate if any generalised spasms, trismus severe, or airway compromise
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - **ICU/HDU** – all confirmed cases: monitor for autonomic storms, muscle spasms, respiratory failure  
  - **Sedation** – titrate benzodiazepines (diazepam or midazolam) ± propofol; consider magnesium sulphate infusion (loading 40 mg/kg, then 1–2 g/h IV to maintain 2–4 mmol/L serum Mg)  
  - **Muscle relaxants** – morphine for sedation and sympathetic blockade; baclofen (oral or intrathecal) if severe  
  - **Autonomic management** – beta-blockers (propranolol, labetalol) for tachycardia/hypertension; atropine for bradycardia; magnesium as first-line  
  - **Nutrition** – nasogastric feeding (or parenteral if ileus); high-calorie, high-protein due to catabolism  
  - **Aspiration prevention** – head-up position, suction, consider PEG if prolonged
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Complete tetanus vaccination series (if not immune): 3 doses of tetanus toxoid (at 0, 1, 6–12 months)  
  - Rehabilitation – physiotherapy, speech therapy (dysphagia), psychological support
- **Default Orders (if admitted – ICU/HDU):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann’s at 1 mL/kg/h (titrate to BP, avoid overload)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: low molecular weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily) – use with caution if bleeding risk from trauma  
  - Diet: NBM until swallowing assessed (aspiration risk); start NG feed once stable  
  - Observations: continuous cardiac monitoring, SpO2, hourly BP, temperature; record NEWS2 every 15 min initially, then 1-4 hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Human tetanus immunoglobulin (HTIG)** – [check local policy] dose for treatment: 3000–6000 IU IV (or IM if IV unavailable); give ASAP  
  - **Tetanus toxoid (adsorbed)** – 0.5 mL IM (deltoid)  
  - **Metronidazole** – 500 mg IV (or PO once able) TDS for 7–10 days  
  - **Benzodiazepines:**  
    - Diazepam 5–10 mg IV bolus (can repeat q10 min), then infusion 5–10 mg/h titrated  
    - Midazolam 1–5 mg IV bolus, then infusion 0.1–0.3 mg/kg/h (ICU only)  
  - **Magnesium sulphate** – 40 mg/kg IV loading over 30 min, then 1–2 g/h infusion; target serum Mg 2–4 mmol/L
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Benzylpenicillin 1.2 g IV QDS (if allergy to metronidazole)  
  - Propofol infusion (ICU sedation)  
  - Baclofen 10–40 mg PO QDS (or intrathecal pump in refractory spasms)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Benzodiazepines – respiratory depression (monitor O2 saturations)  
  - Magnesium – causes hypotension, muscle weakness; contraindicated in myasthenia gravis  
  - Beta-blockers – avoid in acute bradycardia; use labetalol with caution
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Metronidazole: adjust in severe hepatic impairment (reduce dose by 50%)  
  - Magnesium: reduce infusion rate in renal impairment (monitor serum Mg)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving HTIG IM without wound debridement** – toxin still present; debridement is essential before IG works.  
  - **Overdosing benzodiazepines** causing respiratory arrest – always have resuscitation equipment ready.  
  - **Using suxamethonium for RSI** – can cause hyperkalaemia in muscle rigidity states; prefer rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV (if available).

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Respiratory failure (laryngospasm, chest wall rigidity, aspiration pneumonitis)  
  - Autonomic instability (arrhythmias, hypotension, cardiac arrest)  
  - Rhabdomyolysis (leading to AKI)  
  - Fractures (vertebral compression, rib fractures from opisthotonos)  
  - Venous thromboembolism (immobility)  
  - Hyperpyrexia (from sustained muscle activity)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Prolonged ICU stay (weeks to months)  
  - Dysphagia, voice changes  
  - Post‑ICU weakness, tracheostomy dependence in severe cases

### Prognosis
- Mortality: 10–20% overall; higher in elderly, neonates, and those with short incubation period (<7 days).  
- Recovery is slow; survivors often have full recovery but may require months of rehabilitation.  
- Autonomic instability is the leading cause of death.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask about vaccination history** – even if patient says “I’ve had them all,” check the red book or GP records.  
- **Debride the wound thoroughly** – even if it looks clean; remove any foreign body and devitalised tissue.  
- **Start benzodiazepines early** – don’t wait for severe spasms; titrate to relaxation without respiratory depression.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t attribute trismus to dental abscess without examining for generalised rigidity** – you’ll miss tetanus.  
- **Don’t give IM HTIG into the gluteal muscle** – use deltoid or lateral thigh; avoid injecting near identified wound.  
- **Don’t forget active immunisation** – tetanus disease does NOT confer immunity; give toxoid at the same visit.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – only minor wound with unknown vaccination)
- **Return if:**  
  - Any jaw stiffness or difficulty opening mouth  
  - Unexplained muscle aches or stiffness in neck/back  
  - Fever, chills, or new wound erythema/swelling  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP in 2–3 days for wound check and to complete tetanus vaccination series (if incomplete)  
  - If HTIG was given, repeat toxoid at 1 month and 6–12 months (if primary series)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**  
  - Progression of trismus or any generalised spasms  
  - Stridor, respiratory distress, or SpO2 <92% on room air  
  - Autonomic instability: BP >160/100 or <90/60, heart rate >130 or <50, temperature >39°C  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single parameter ≥3)  
- **Call crash team:**  
  - Cardiac arrest, severe bradycardia, or hypotension unresponsive to fluids  
  - Suspected laryngospasm with airway obstruction  
- **Specific thresholds:**  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (if available)  
  - GCS <14 (rare, but can occur from sedation or hypoxia)
